stories.headline: Nuggets defeat Timberwolves 125-113 in Game 5, extend series
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets staved off elimination Monday night with a 125-113 victory over the Minnesota Timberwolves at Ball Arena, extending their first-round NBA playoff series to a Game 6. The win keeps Denver's season alive after the team had fallen into a 3-1 series deficit following three consecutive losses.

Nikola Jokic made postseason history during the third quarter, recording his 10th rebound of the game to tie former teammate Russell Westbrook for the most career triple-doubles when combining regular-season and postseason statistics. The milestone came on a night when Denver needed its franchise player to deliver under intense pressure from a hostile home crowd hungry for results.

Game 6 is scheduled for Thursday in Minnesota. The Nuggets entered the series as one of the favorites to reach the NBA Finals but have struggled with inconsistent shooting throughout, with Jokic shooting just 39.1 percent overall in the series and supporting players Jamal Murray and Christian Braun also underperforming.

Forward Aaron Gordon watched Game 5 from the bench in street clothes, his absence adding another layer of concern for Denver's playoff hopes. A win Thursday would force a decisive Game 7 back at Ball Arena.

stories.headline: Anti-violence advocate convicted of second-degree murder in Commerce City birthday party shooting
stories.summary:
A jury in Adams County District Court convicted Lumumba Sayers Sr., 47, of second-degree murder Monday in the August 2024 fatal shooting of 28-year-old Malcolm Watson at a pool birthday party for Watson's son at Pioneer Park in Commerce City. Sayers Sr. had been charged with first-degree murder but was convicted of the lesser charge, which carries a typical sentence of 16 to 48 years in prison. He was also found guilty of tampering with evidence but acquitted of menacing.

Prosecutors argued that Sayers Sr. — who ran the Heavy Hands Heavy Hearts Center, an anti-violence organization in Aurora — killed Watson to avenge the death of his own son, Lumumba Sayers Jr., who was killed in a Five Points shootout in 2023. Sayers Sr. allegedly believed Watson was connected to his son's death. Surveillance video showed Sayers Sr. walk directly up to Watson at the party and shoot him at close range.

The defense, led by attorney Megan Downing, argued that another man present at the scene, referred to as "Rico," was the actual shooter, pointing to witnesses who reported seeing a man in a tan tracksuit flee with an object in his pocket. Prosecutors countered that Sayers Sr. was seen on video brandishing an unregistered handgun immediately after the killing and appeared to plant the weapon near Watson's body.

Jurors received the case Thursday, deliberated through Friday and into Monday morning, and returned their verdict around 12:40 p.m. Sentencing is scheduled for July 24.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers kill lot-size reform bill as legislative session nears end
stories.summary:
The Colorado Senate Local Government and Housing Committee voted 7-0 Monday to kill House Bill 1114, which would have prohibited local governments from requiring single-family home lots larger than 2,000 square feet. The bill had passed the House in late February but stalled in the Senate, which has been more skeptical of zoning reform measures. Senate sponsor Matt Ball, a Denver Democrat, requested the bill's euthanization following testimony and debate.

Despite the defeat, a related housing measure remains alive. House Bill 1308, which would allow homeowners to sell off a smaller section of their lot generally without local government approval, is scheduled for a Senate committee hearing this week. Sixteen days remain in the 2026 legislative session.

Several other major policy debates are also reaching critical stages. A bill rewriting Colorado's artificial intelligence regulations is expected to be introduced this week, and the 2026 budget bill is receiving final adjustments. Legislation related to the Cover All Coloradans program for immigrant children and pregnant women is also in its final stages.

Additionally, the House is set to vote on House Bill 1276, which would limit Colorado officials' cooperation with federal immigration authorities, and House Bill 1335, which would generally require universities to offer abortion medication at campus health centers.

stories.headline: Real Brokerage acquires Denver-based Re/Max in $880 million deal, headquarters moving to Miami
stories.summary:
Real Brokerage announced it will acquire Denver-based Re/Max in an $880 million transaction that will consolidate the two real estate companies under a Miami headquarters. Re/Max, which has been headquartered in Denver since its founding, will be absorbed into the Real Brokerage operation while significant Denver-area operations are expected to continue.

The deal combines two major residential real estate brokerages at a time of ongoing turbulence in the industry, which has faced pressure from commission structure changes following recent national legal settlements. Re/Max has long been one of Denver's most prominent corporate anchors in the real estate sector.

Details about the impact on Re/Max's existing Denver workforce and local office footprint were not immediately available. The transaction represents one of the larger corporate acquisitions involving a Denver-headquartered company in recent years.

The announcement comes as Denver's commercial real estate market continues to face headwinds, with downtown office vacancy rates at elevated levels and the broader industry adjusting to shifts in how properties are bought and sold.

stories.headline: Denver City Council kicks off 2027 budget season with focus on public safety, housing, basic services
stories.summary:
Denver's City Council opened the 2027 budget planning cycle Monday during a Budget and Policy Committee meeting, with members outlining priorities centered on public safety, housing, homelessness, infrastructure and restoring basic city services. The council identified eight broad budget priority areas but did not formally rank individual spending preferences. Formal budget pitches will be made at a council workshop scheduled for May 14.

Among the most frequently cited priorities were restoring funding to the Denver Police Crime Lab, bolstering police and sheriff department staffing, and expanding the STAR program, a civilian crisis response initiative that dispatches mental health clinicians and paramedics rather than officers to low-risk 911 calls.

Councilmember Kevin Flynn emphasized the need to restore staffing at basic city services including the Department of Motor Vehicles, parking magistrates and the Department of Finance. Flynn noted the DMV generates tens of millions of dollars in annual revenue for the city and that residents have faced service delays and closures.

Council members also called for increased investment in housing and family homelessness services, particularly for families with children, as well as expanded funding for roads, bridges and transportation infrastructure positions.

stories.headline: Denver transitions to Veo as city's sole scooter and e-bike provider under new contract
stories.summary:
Denver City Council unanimously approved a new micromobility contract Monday with California-based Veo, replacing current providers Lime and Bird. The contract begins May 1 and runs at least three years. Bird is expected to remove all scooters from Denver streets by Friday, while Lime has until May 15 to remove its devices.

Veo will charge Denver residents a $1 unlock fee plus 25 cents per minute, down from the current general rate of 44 cents per minute for all users. Non-residents will pay 39 cents per minute. The company will deploy sidewalk-detection technology to warn riders against sidewalk riding, and users must photograph their parked scooter at the end of each ride to confirm it does not block pedestrians.

The contract came after weeks of controversy, with a council committee delaying consideration twice before allowing the full council to vote. Council members ultimately approved it in a block vote. Veo's app is already available for download, and the company also offers a phone number to unlock devices.

Low-income riders, veterans, military members, Medicare and Medicaid enrollees, RTD discount pass holders and students receiving financial aid can qualify for an hour of free riding per day under Veo's access program.

stories.headline: AI license plate reader cameras spread across Colorado, sparking privacy pushback
stories.summary:
Automated license plate reader cameras made by Flock Safety have been installed across Colorado through contracts with nearly 100 law enforcement agencies and more than 160 private entities, including homeowners associations, according to a Colorado Sun investigation. The cameras, roughly the size of a soda can and mounted on traffic infrastructure, photograph passing vehicles and store license plate data along with time, date and location information.

A grassroots group called Eyes Off Colorado has been mapping the cameras' locations and organizing public demonstrations to raise awareness of the surveillance network. Volunteers gathered in March at locations across the state — including Firestone and Golden — to point out cameras to passing motorists, many of whom were unaware the devices existed.

Law enforcement agencies say the technology helps solve crimes and recover stolen vehicles. Critics counter that the system builds a searchable record of residents' daily movements and that data can be stored for weeks or months, potentially shared with federal agencies including immigration enforcement.

Colorado lawmakers have introduced Senate Bills 70 and 71 this session to establish guardrails on the technology, including limits on data retention, warrant requirements for certain searches and audit and penalty provisions for misuse. The legislation is still working through the Capitol.

stories.headline: Colorado Supreme Court weighs ski resort liability waivers in Epic Pass case
stories.summary:
The Colorado Supreme Court heard oral arguments this month in a case examining whether a skier waived his right to sue Vail Resorts when he purchased an Epic Pass roughly two years after being injured in a collision with an employee-driven snowmobile at Breckenridge ski area in December 2020. Snowboarder John Litterer, a Texas resident, argues he did not knowingly surrender his pending lawsuit when he clicked through the six-page Epic Pass purchase agreement in November 2022.

The case comes in the wake of the court's 2024 ruling in the Annie Miller case, which found that scroll-and-click liability waivers cannot always shield ski areas from negligence claims. Litterer's attorney argued that the Epic Pass language, while broad, was not sufficiently plain to put his client on notice that he was waiving an active lawsuit.

Chief Justice Monica Márquez questioned how the language would not have put Litterer on notice, noting he was already aware of his own litigation. The contract included a release of "any and all claims" including "anything which has happened up to now," which Márquez described as "pretty plain language."

The case has significant implications for the ski industry across Colorado, where liability waivers are standard practice. Litterer's attorney told the court that the legislature has not acted on recent attempts to enhance skier safety laws, making the court's ruling the primary vehicle for establishing standards.

stories.headline: One person killed after fuel tanker crashes and catches fire on South Santa Fe Drive in Englewood
stories.summary:
A commercial fuel tanker truck crashed and caught fire Monday morning on South Santa Fe Drive in Englewood, killing one person and creating a hazmat situation that shut down the roadway for hours. South Metro Fire Rescue responded to the initial call around 5:38 a.m. near a U-Haul facility between West Belleview Avenue and West Union Avenue.

Firefighters chose not to apply water to the flames due to concerns about environmental runoff from the fuel, instead allowing the fire to burn itself out. Nearby structures were evacuated as a precaution given the tanker's proximity to buildings, though broader neighborhood evacuations were not ordered. The fire spread to nearby grass but was characterized as minor.

The Denver Fire Department, Littleton Police Department and Englewood Police Department all assisted South Metro Fire Rescue at the scene. Santa Fe Drive was closed in all directions between Belleview and Union avenues while crews managed the scene and investigated.

The identity of the fatality was not immediately released. Once the fire extinguished, police took over the investigation into the circumstances of the crash.

stories.headline: Loveland distillery's Colorado whiskey named world's best at London Spirits Competition
stories.summary:
Root Shoot Spirits, a Loveland-based distillery, won the title of Whisky of the Year at the ninth annual London Spirits Competition, beating out entries from around the globe with its American Single Malt Whiskey. Judges awarded the spirit a double-gold medal with 97 out of 100 points, evaluating it on aroma, flavor, body, value and shelf appeal. The whiskey also earned American Single Malt Whiskey of the Year and Spirit of the Year U.S.A. honors.

Root Shoot Spirits is a sister company to Root Shoot Malting, owned by fifth-generation farmer Todd Olander, who grows approximately 700 acres of Colorado barley that is kilned and roasted for use by brewers and distillers throughout the state. The American Single Malt Whiskey, made entirely from Colorado-grown grain, was first released in 2023. It previously won the best American-made single malt category at the same competition in 2025.

"You can't recognize Root Shoot Whiskey without also acknowledging the power of Colorado agriculture — from the high altitude, to the soil, to the people working the land," Olander said in a statement. "This is a special place that produces special malts and whiskies."

Denver's Stranahan's distillery also performed well in the competition, earning a double-gold medal for Stranahan's Original and gold medals for four other expressions. Colorado has long been recognized for its craft beer industry, and the results suggest the state's distilling sector is achieving similar international recognition.

stories.headline: Injured backcountry skier rescued from James Peak Wilderness Area near Winter Park
stories.summary:
Search and rescue teams recovered an injured and immobilized backcountry skier from the James Peak Wilderness Area on Saturday after receiving a 911 call at approximately 12:18 p.m., according to the Gilpin County Sheriff's Office. The skier was located about five miles northeast of Winter Park in rugged, mountainous terrain near Forest Lake, part of a group of three.

A satellite SOS device carried by the injured party proved crucial in pinpointing the group's location, supplementing a drone search effort that was hampered by tree cover and vast wilderness terrain. Rescuers made contact with the injured skier by approximately 3:49 p.m., and all three skiers were successfully extracted from the backcountry by 7:44 p.m.

The operation involved multiple agencies and volunteer organizations, including Alpine Rescue Team, Rocky Mountain Rescue Group, Timberline Fire Protection District and Gilpin Ambulance Authority. The sheriff's office credited the satellite communication device as a key factor in the successful and timely outcome.

The incident is a reminder to backcountry travelers that the Colorado ski season can extend well into late April, and that satellite communication devices significantly improve rescue response times in areas beyond cell service.

stories.headline: Late-season snow possible in Denver Monday night as spring storm system moves through
stories.summary:
Denver could see a few snowflakes Monday night as temperatures drop following a spring storm system, according to Fox31 meteorologist Travis Michels. Accumulation chances are considered low, and any snow would likely represent one of the final snow events of the season for the metro area.

Arapahoe Basin ski area announced its closing date following what the resort characterized as the longest Colorado ski season on record, coinciding with the late-spring weather pattern. The timing of the potential snow underscores the extended winter conditions that have persisted across the Colorado high country.

Denver's spring weather has been unsettled in recent weeks, with scattered showers and cooler temperatures interrupting the seasonal transition. Residents are advised to check forecasts before outdoor evening plans Monday.

joke.joke: I like a woman with a head on her shoulders. I hate necks.

topStory.headline: Armed Man Opens Fire Outside White House Correspondents' Dinner, Citing Anti-Trump Motive
topStory.summary:
An armed California man opened fire outside the White House Correspondents' Association dinner Saturday night at the Washington Hilton, where President Donald Trump and other administration officials had gathered with journalists for the annual black-tie gala. Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, a teacher and engineer, rushed a security checkpoint armed with a shotgun, a handgun and knives before being shot and tackled to the ground by law enforcement.

Authorities say Allen traveled by train from Los Angeles to Chicago, then on to Washington, D.C., where he checked into the hotel in the days before the attack. Approximately ten minutes before opening fire, Allen sent family members a written note apologizing to his parents, colleagues, students and bystanders, while also outlining what he called his 'expected rules of engagement,' naming Trump administration officials as targets prioritized by rank. He explicitly exempted FBI Director Kash Patel. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche confirmed the broad outlines of the attack on NBC's 'Meet the Press' on Sunday.

Allen's brother contacted the New London Police Department in Connecticut at approximately 10:49 p.m. Saturday after receiving the note, and was subsequently interviewed by federal authorities. Allen's sister separately told the Secret Service and Montgomery County police that her brother had a history of making radical statements and had referenced a plan to do 'something' to address what he saw as problems in the world. A Bluesky social media account believed to belong to Allen contained recent posts critical of Trump and his administration's policies.

The shooting rapidly spawned a wave of online conspiracy theories, with the word 'staged' trending on X within hours and speculation spreading across Reddit and Instagram. Despite swift, evidence-backed reporting from journalists who attended the event and a clear law enforcement account of events, baseless theories alleging the incident was fabricated spread widely. President Trump himself noted the speed of the speculation in a Sunday interview with CBS's '60 Minutes,' observing that in past incidents it typically took months for such theories to emerge. Experts and commentators across the political spectrum criticized the misinformation, with a White House spokesman calling those who believed Trump staged the attack 'complete morons.'
